title: Machine learning classification
summary: Train classifiers, interpret confusion-matrix tradeoffs, and choose metrics that match the real cost of mistakes.

keywords: A QUICK LOOK AT MACHINE LEARNING

items.prompt: What is the core difference between precision and recall?

solution.summary: Precision focuses on how many predicted positives are correct, while recall focuses on how many actual positives were found.

items.prompt: Which metric becomes especially important when missing a real positive case is costly?

choices.label: Precision only because it is always best

solution.summary: When missing real positives is expensive, recall matters because it tracks how many positives you successfully catch.

items.prompt: Why can accuracy be misleading on an imbalanced dataset?
acceptedAnswers: a trivial model can look good

solution.summary: A model can achieve high accuracy by predicting only the majority class while completely failing on the minority class.

items.prompt: Fit a logistic regression classifier on the tiny dataset below and print the prediction for `[3]`.
items.starterCode:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
X = [[0], [1], [2], [4], [5]]
y = [0, 0, 0, 1, 1]
# fit and print the prediction for [3]

pyodide.tests:
import io, contextlib
buf = io.StringIO()
with contextlib.redirect_stdout(buf):
    exec(user_code, {})
assert buf.getvalue().strip() in {'0', '1'}, 'Prediction should be a class label.'
__grade_result__ = {'passed': True, 'message': 'Your classifier trains and predicts successfully.'}

solution.summary: Use the reference solution to compare structure, not just syntax.
solution.code:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
X = [[0], [1], [2], [4], [5]]
y = [0, 0, 0, 1, 1]
model = LogisticRegression().fit(X, y)
print(int(model.predict([[3]])[0]))
